---
title: "Oral Code Checks: Guidelines"
when: "Week 4 and Week 5"
summary: "What to expect and how to prepare"
url_signup: "https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1kEhEwLhfhyi4_VdysEGjLAA8jFzczVQfn3oKrRFYE_4/edit?usp=sharing"
url_rubric: "https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/17Qd6Pb_lgS7i6gHu1JTQJ-PXhV7uV0BkizidNGQ2KSg/edit?usp=sharing"
page-layout: full
draft: false
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE)
```

## Info and Sign-ups

- **When**: {{< meta when >}}
- **Format**: 15-minute 1:1 conversation via Zoom [Use the OHs links from Home](../index.qmd)
- **Sign-up Sheet**: [Book your Slot]({{< meta url_signup >}})
- **Rubric**: [View the Rubric]({{< meta url_rubric >}})


## Scheduling Policy

All checks must be scheduled (first come, first served) **by Sunday 7/5 using the sign up spreadsheet linked at the top of this page**. If you haven't picked a slot by then, I'll assign you one from what's left, so it's worth signing up early to get a time that works best for you. 

Rescheduling isn't guaranteed, and will only be considered for documented circumstances or emergencies: reach out ASAP and explain what's going on if that happens. Regardless, oral code checks cannot be scheduled after Week 5. 

All current slots fall during class time throughout Week 4 and Week 5. **Before the oral code checks start on a given day, we will have our regular lecture.** For example, on Monday of Week 4, we'll review Week 3 materials from 9:30–10:30 AM before starting the oral code checks; on Tuesday of Week 4, there is regular class from 9:30–11:00 AM, etc. In general, this means that regular class will run from 9:30–11:00 AM during Weeks 4 and 5, unless otherwise noted. I will make the best use of our time, and we won't cut any of the scheduled course content.


## Format and Rules

Each student will sign up for one 15-minute conversation with the instructors about their own work in the course:

- **The check runs on Zoom**: Use the Zoom link for the instructor listed in your slot: both [Sabrina's and Ruben's Zoom links are on the course homepage](https://brinasab.github.io/css-website-summer/)
- **I will share my screen**: you won't be asked to share yours. I'll pull up everything we need (your repo with homework, quizzes, in-class exercises, etc.).
- **Camera on** for the full session; your torso and hands must be visible at all times.
- You may **not touch your screen, keyboard, or any other device** during the check.

Violating these rules disqualifies you from the check and results in a score of zero.  Please **review the rubric linked at the top of this page** before your meeting so you know what to expect and how you will be evaluated.


## Questions You Can Expect

**Questions will draw from three areas:**

1. **Take-home homework assignments:** one or two questions on the code you wrote, with follow-ups
2. **Weekly quizzes:** one or two questions, with follow-ups
3. **In-class code:** one question from the in-class code covered up until the day of your scheduled check (e.g., this the code you've been downloading during lectures with the purple `usethis::` command); we focus on the in-class code explicitly covered during lectures

**Some questions will be broad, for example:**

- "Walk me through your code / logic here"
- "How did you break this problem down into steps? Why?"

**Others will be more specific, for example:**

- "Why did you choose this approach instead of another one?"
- "What does this piece of code do?"
- "How else could you write this to get the same result, based on what we covered in class?"
- "Why is this the right answer on the quiz?"


## How to Prepare

Review your take-home homework assignments, quizzes, all lecture slides, and in-class code. Make sure you understand each piece and can **explain it out loud**.

The goal is to see your thinking and your familiarity with your own code and the class materials, not to trick you. I won't ask about things we haven't covered in class, and I won't ask you to code live. I also won't ask about the readings directly, though skimming the readings is part of the expected course workload and will help you understand the material better.
